1.11.a.info:
The 2.4 GHz ISM band in the US (FCC regulatory domain) runs from 2.400 GHz to 2.4835 GHz and is divided into 11 channels, numbered 1 through 11. Each channel is 22 MHz wide but the center frequencies are only spaced 5 MHz apart. That mismatch is the entire problem: a 22 MHz signal placed on channel 1 spills into channels 2, 3, 4, and 5 before it fully decays.

Do the math: channel 1 center = 2.412 GHz, channel 6 center = 2.437 GHz (25 MHz gap), channel 11 center = 2.462 GHz (another 25 MHz gap). A 25 MHz spacing is just enough to clear a 22 MHz-wide signal, which is why 1, 6, and 11 are the only three non-overlapping channels in the US 2.4 GHz band. Any other choice — channel 3, channel 9, even channel 4 — overlaps two neighbors and creates adjacent-channel interference (ACI).

When you design a 2.4 GHz WLAN, you assign 1, 6, and 11 to adjacent APs in a honeycomb pattern so no two neighbors share a channel. If you only have three choices and a fourth AP is nearby, the fourth reuses one of the three — at that point you accept some co-channel interference (CCI), which is less destructive than ACI because co-channel APs at least cooperate via CSMA/CA airtime sharing.

Exam trap: some regulatory domains (ETSI/EU) allow channels 1-13, and Japan allows channel 14 for DSSS only. The CCNA uses the FCC model — stick with 1, 6, 11. If a question mentions channels 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9, or 10 as a design choice, it is wrong.

hack.memory: 'One, Six, Eleven — Wi-Fi heaven.' Three numbers, 5 apart, spaced 25 MHz on the dial so their 22 MHz signals don't collide. Burn the math into memory: 22 wide + 5 apart = overlap unless you jump 5 channels at a time. Anything else on the 2.4 GHz dial creates adjacent-channel interference (ACI), which is WORSE than co-channel interference (CCI).
hack.practice: On paper, draw the 2.4 GHz spectrum from 2.400 to 2.483 GHz as a bar. Mark channels 1-11 at 5 MHz intervals. Shade each channel as a 22 MHz-wide block and watch channels 2-5 bleed into both 1 and 6. Then stack only 1, 6, 11 and confirm no overlap. In Packet Tracer, deploy 3-4 APs and manually assign 1, 6, 11 in a honeycomb. Explain out loud why any other channel is a deployment mistake.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55 (Wireless Fundamentals). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Almost every CCNA wireless question set has a '1, 6, 11' question — it is the single most predictable wireless fact on the exam.

1.11.a.info:
The 5 GHz band (technically 5.150-5.825 GHz, split across UNII-1, UNII-2, UNII-2 Extended, and UNII-3 sub-bands) was designed with Wi-Fi in mind. Unlike 2.4 GHz, the 5 GHz channels are spaced so they do not overlap at the default 20 MHz width. In the US regulatory domain, you get 24+ non-overlapping 20 MHz channels — numbers like 36, 40, 44, 48, 52, 56, 60, 64, 100, 104, ..., 144, 149, 153, 157, 161, 165.

Why so many more channels? The 5 GHz band is simply wider (675 MHz of usable spectrum vs. 83.5 MHz at 2.4 GHz), and channel numbering jumps by 4 (centers 20 MHz apart), so each 20 MHz channel sits in its own clean slot. There is no 'channel 3 problem' the way there is at 2.4 GHz.

More channels means less co-channel interference (CCI) in dense deployments, because every adjacent AP can be given a unique channel. Many of the middle channels (52-144) are DFS (Dynamic Frequency Selection) channels — they are shared with weather and military radar, and the AP must listen for radar pulses and vacate the channel if one is detected. Consumer APs often skip DFS channels; enterprise APs use them to unlock more capacity.

Tradeoff: the 5 GHz signal attenuates faster through walls and over distance, so coverage range is shorter than 2.4 GHz. You solve that with more APs, not more power.

Exam takeaway: 5 GHz = 24+ non-overlapping 20 MHz channels, far cleaner than 2.4 GHz, but shorter range. This is THE advantage of 5 GHz and it will be tested.

hack.memory: '5 GHz = 5x the channels (actually 8x).' 24+ non-overlapping vs. 3 at 2.4 GHz. Channels go up by 4 (36, 40, 44, 48...) because each is 20 MHz wide and centers are 20 MHz apart — no overlap math to memorize. Trade: cleaner spectrum, shorter range.
hack.practice: Flashcard: '2.4 GHz = __ non-overlapping channels, 5 GHz = __ non-overlapping channels.' Answers: 3 and 24+. List the low UNII-1 channels you see on every home router: 36, 40, 44, 48. Then the high UNII-3: 149, 153, 157, 161, 165. In a WLC lab or your home router, open the 5 GHz channel dropdown and count how many choices you have versus the 2.4 GHz dropdown.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. CCNA does not drill specific 5 GHz channel numbers, but it expects you to know the 5 GHz band has many more non-overlapping channels than 2.4 GHz. That single fact is testable.

1.11.a.info:
Channel bonding combines adjacent 20 MHz channels into a single wider channel to increase throughput. In the 5 GHz band, you can bond to 20, 40, 80, or 160 MHz widths (Wi-Fi 5 supports up to 80 natively and 160 optionally; Wi-Fi 6 supports 160 widely).

The math is linear: doubling channel width roughly doubles theoretical throughput, because you have twice the spectrum to carry OFDM subcarriers. An 80 MHz channel can push 4x the data of a 20 MHz channel under ideal conditions.

The cost is the number of non-overlapping channels. If you use 20 MHz widths, you get 24+ non-overlapping channels. Bond to 40 MHz and you cut that roughly in half. Bond to 80 MHz and you have maybe 6 non-overlapping channels. Bond to 160 MHz and you have 2 non-overlapping channels — nearly unusable in dense deployments because every AP interferes with every other AP.

Design rule: use wider channels in sparse, high-throughput environments (conference rooms, point-to-point links, home networks with few neighbors); use narrower channels (20 or 40 MHz) in dense enterprise deployments where channel reuse matters more than raw per-AP throughput.

Wi-Fi 7 / 802.11be extends bonding to 320 MHz channels, but that is beyond CCNA scope. For the exam: know that 5 GHz supports 20/40/80/160 MHz widths, and wider = faster but fewer non-overlapping channels.

hack.memory: 'Wider pipe, fewer pipes.' Bonding doubles throughput at every step (20 → 40 → 80 → 160 MHz) but halves the number of non-overlapping channels. Home network with 2 neighbors = go wide. Enterprise with 40 APs per floor = stay narrow.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'If you bond 4 x 20 MHz channels, what width and how many non-overlapping channels remain at 5 GHz?' Answer: 80 MHz, about 6. In your home router admin page, change the 5 GHz channel width from 20 → 40 → 80 MHz and watch the channel dropdown shrink. Observe the theoretical max data rate climb.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. The exam may ask which channel width gives more throughput vs. more channel reuse — know the tradeoff, not the exact numbers.

1.11.a.info:
Co-channel interference (CCI) happens when two or more APs (or clients) are configured on the exact same channel within radio range of each other. Because they share a channel, they share the airtime, and CSMA/CA forces them to take turns transmitting.

Effect: each AP's effective throughput is roughly cut in half — two APs on channel 6 have to alternate, so neither sees the full 100% of the channel's airtime. Three APs on channel 6? Each gets roughly a third. This is why a poorly planned 2.4 GHz network with four APs all on channel 1 feels sluggish even when signal strength looks fine.

CCI is NOT the same as a collision. Because CSMA/CA detects energy on the channel before transmitting (carrier sense), the APs and clients take polite turns — they don't smash into each other, they just wait. Wait time = lost throughput.

How to mitigate: assign non-overlapping channels to neighbors. In 2.4 GHz, alternate 1, 6, 11 in a honeycomb pattern so no two adjacent APs share a channel. In 5 GHz, pick any 4 non-overlapping channels and rotate them. If you run out of channels (unavoidable in dense deployments), CCI is the preferred evil vs. ACI — at least CCI shares airtime cooperatively.

Diagnosing CCI: a Wi-Fi analyzer (NetSpot, Cisco Spectrum Expert, WLC 'Air Quality' reports) will show multiple BSSIDs on the same channel with overlapping coverage. High channel utilization (>50%) combined with multiple same-channel neighbors is the classic signature.

hack.memory: CCI = 'same lane, take turns.' Two cars in one lane on a one-lane road cooperate — nobody crashes, but you move at half speed. Bad, but not catastrophic. ACI is the real disaster (see next entry).
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Two APs on channel 6, same coverage area — what is the effective throughput per AP?' Answer: roughly 50%. Then: 'Is CCI better or worse than ACI?' Answer: better — CCI cooperates, ACI collides. In Packet Tracer or a real deployment, put two APs on the same channel and run speedtest on each client simultaneously. Watch throughput drop on both.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. The CCI/ACI distinction is a favorite exam trap. Know that CCI is preferred over ACI in channel planning.

1.11.a.info:
Adjacent-channel interference (ACI) happens when two APs are on overlapping but not identical channels — for example, one AP on channel 1 and another on channel 3 in the 2.4 GHz band. Because channel 1 occupies 2.401-2.423 GHz and channel 3 occupies 2.411-2.433 GHz, they share ~12 MHz of spectrum.

Why ACI is worse than CCI: CSMA/CA does not recognize the overlapping AP as being on the 'same channel.' So the two APs do NOT take turns. Both transmit simultaneously, and their signals physically corrupt each other in the overlap region. From each AP's perspective, the other AP's signal looks like random noise, which raises the noise floor and degrades the signal-to-noise ratio for everyone.

The result is not just lost throughput — it's actual data corruption. Frames get retransmitted, clients see degraded throughput even with strong RSSI, and the network feels unreliable rather than just slow. This is why a 2.4 GHz deployment using channels 1, 3, 6, 8, 11 is worse than a deployment using only 1, 6, 11 even though the 'more channels' intuition feels right.

Mitigation: stick to non-overlapping channels only — 1, 6, 11 in 2.4 GHz. If you must repeat a channel, repeat with CCI (same channel) rather than ACI (overlapping channel). The entire point of channel planning is to eliminate ACI and minimize CCI.

ACI does not exist at 5 GHz when using 20 MHz channels, because the 5 GHz channel grid is designed so adjacent channels do not overlap. ACI only reappears when channel bonding creates wider channels that are placed too close together.

hack.memory: ACI = 'two cars side-by-side in the same lane, neither sees the other.' Instead of taking turns (CCI), they sideswipe. That is why ACI is worse: no cooperation, just corruption. The fix is always 1, 6, 11 — never pick a channel between.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Why is ACI worse than CCI?' Answer: 'CSMA/CA cannot carrier-sense overlapping channels, so both APs transmit at once and their signals corrupt each other — no airtime sharing.' In Packet Tracer, configure two APs on channels 1 and 3. Run a ping with large payloads and high frequency. Observe retransmissions.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. If the exam gives you a scenario with channel 3 or channel 9 anywhere in the design, the answer involves fixing it to 1, 6, or 11.

1.11.a.info:
A honeycomb channel plan is the standard layout for 2.4 GHz AP deployments. You assign channels 1, 6, and 11 to adjacent APs in a hexagonal (honeycomb) pattern so that no two touching APs share a channel. This maximizes the reuse of the three available non-overlapping channels.

Visualize it as tiles: imagine covering a floor plan with hexagonal AP coverage zones. Color each hex with 1, 6, or 11 so adjacent hexes are always different. In a well-planned 2.4 GHz floor, every AP's nearest neighbors use the other two channels. Only APs that are two hops away may reuse the same channel — at that distance, signal attenuation makes them invisible to each other, so CCI is minimal.

In 5 GHz, you have 24+ non-overlapping channels, so you do not need a strict honeycomb — you can give every adjacent AP its own unique channel and still have plenty left over. Enterprise deployments often use Cisco RRM (Radio Resource Management) to automate the channel selection: the WLC monitors the RF environment and assigns channels dynamically so neighbors never clash.

Practical limits: with only three 2.4 GHz channels, any floor with more than three AP columns will inevitably repeat channels. That is why enterprise designs lean on 5 GHz for capacity (multiple channels) and use 2.4 GHz for coverage (longer range). A honeycomb on 2.4 GHz is 'best effort, minimize CCI'; a proper channel plan on 5 GHz is 'unique channel per AP.'

Transmit power matters too: lower TX power shrinks each AP's cell, reducing the overlap area where CCI or ACI can happen. RRM tunes both channel AND power for each AP.

hack.memory: Honeycomb = bee hive. Each hex (AP) touches six neighbors. With three colors (1, 6, 11), no two touching hexes share a color. Tile it on a napkin once and you'll never forget the pattern. 5 GHz doesn't need a honeycomb because it has 24+ colors.
hack.practice: On graph paper, draw a grid of 12 hexagons. Label each 1, 6, or 11 so no adjacent pair matches. Reproduce from memory until it clicks. In Packet Tracer, drop 4 APs on a floor plan diagram and assign 1, 6, 11, 1 — verify no two adjacent APs share a channel.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. You will see diagrams on the exam asking 'which channel should AP-D use?' The answer is always the one that avoids matching adjacent APs.

1.11.a.info:
The 2.4 GHz ISM band is shared with many non-Wi-Fi devices, and each one adds noise to the spectrum. The most common interferers:

  • Microwave ovens — operate at ~2.45 GHz (right in the middle of the band). A running microwave is detectable as a broad noise spike on channels 6-11. This is the classic 'lunch-hour Wi-Fi problem' in office break rooms.
  • Bluetooth devices — use frequency-hopping spread spectrum (FHSS) across 79 x 1 MHz channels within the 2.4 GHz band. Bluetooth constantly bounces across the band, creating intermittent noise everywhere. Newer AFH (Adaptive Frequency Hopping) avoids active Wi-Fi channels, but legacy Bluetooth does not.
  • Baby monitors, wireless cameras, and cordless phones — older analog models blast continuous narrow-band signals on specific 2.4 GHz frequencies, acting as persistent jammers on whatever channel they are on.
  • Zigbee / IoT devices — use 802.15.4, which overlaps several 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi channels with its own 16 narrow channels.

Why 5 GHz is cleaner: almost none of these devices use 5 GHz. Microwaves, Bluetooth, and most IoT protocols are locked to 2.4 GHz. The 5 GHz band is shared mainly with radar (on DFS channels) and other Wi-Fi.

Diagnosing interference requires a spectrum analyzer — Cisco's CleanAir APs include this as a hardware feature. A regular Wi-Fi scanner shows other Wi-Fi, but only a spectrum analyzer shows microwave leakage or an unknown jammer on the raw RF.

Exam takeaway: know that 2.4 GHz suffers from more non-Wi-Fi interference than 5 GHz. That is a real, testable selling point for 5 GHz beyond just 'more channels.'

hack.memory: 2.4 GHz = 'the junk drawer of radio.' Everything cheap lives there: microwaves, Bluetooth, baby monitors, garage doors, Zigbee. 5 GHz = 'the VIP room' — almost nothing else on the dial. That is a real reason to prefer 5 GHz, not just 'more channels.'
hack.practice: Flashcard list: 'Name 4 non-Wi-Fi interferers on the 2.4 GHz band.' Answers: microwave ovens, Bluetooth, baby monitors, cordless phones. Bonus: Zigbee/802.15.4. Run a Wi-Fi analyzer app on your phone next to a running microwave — watch the 2.4 GHz noise floor rise.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Expect one question like 'why is 5 GHz cleaner?' — the answer combines 'more non-overlapping channels' with 'fewer non-Wi-Fi interferers.'

1.11.a.info:
Radio Resource Management (RRM) is a Cisco WLC feature that automatically manages the RF environment across all APs in a WLAN. Instead of manually assigning channels and transmit power to each AP, the WLC's RRM engine continuously monitors the RF via neighbor reports from each AP and adjusts settings to minimize interference and maximize coverage.

RRM has three main sub-features:

  • DCA (Dynamic Channel Assignment) — picks the best channel for each AP based on neighbor interference, noise, and channel utilization. If an AP's channel gets noisy (e.g., a new neighbor appears), DCA migrates that AP to a cleaner channel.
  • TPC (Transmit Power Control) — adjusts each AP's TX power up or down so cells are sized correctly. If two APs are too close (strong signal heard from each other), TPC lowers both. If an AP loses a neighbor (coverage hole), TPC raises surrounding APs to fill in.
  • CHD (Coverage Hole Detection) — spots weak-signal areas where clients are connecting at low data rates and triggers TPC increases to fix coverage.

All three run via neighbor discovery: every AP listens for RF messages from every other AP in the group, builds an 'RF neighbor list,' and reports it to the WLC. The WLC uses these reports to build a global picture of the RF environment and optimize the network as a whole.

Why this matters: manual channel planning is fine for 3-5 APs. In a campus with 200 APs, RRM is the only practical way to keep the RF clean as offices rearrange, APs are added, and neighboring tenants change. Enterprise WLANs rely on it.

hack.memory: RRM = 'autopilot for Wi-Fi channels and power.' Three engines: DCA (channels), TPC (power), CHD (coverage holes). Every AP rats out its neighbors to the WLC, and the WLC tunes the whole network like a DJ balancing a crowd.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'What does RRM do, and name its three main sub-features?' Answer: DCA = Dynamic Channel Assignment, TPC = Transmit Power Control, CHD = Coverage Hole Detection. If you have access to a Cisco WLC lab (Packet Tracer or DevNet Sandbox), find the RRM tab under Wireless > 802.11a/b/g/n and inspect the DCA and TPC settings.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56 (Wireless Architectures) covers WLC features including RRM. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 26. RRM is name-dropped on the exam — knowing it does 'auto channel and power' on a Cisco WLC is enough.

1.11.b.info:
The SSID (Service Set Identifier) is the human-readable name of a wireless network — what you see in the Wi-Fi list on your phone ('Home-WiFi', 'Starbucks Guest', 'Corp-WLAN'). Up to 32 bytes of any character. It is configured on the AP or WLC and advertised to clients via beacon frames, typically sent every ~100 ms on the AP's active channel.

When a client wants to discover networks, it listens for beacons (passive scan) or actively sends probe requests asking 'who is out there?' (active scan). APs respond with probe responses that include the SSID. Either way, the SSID is how the client and AP agree on 'which network are we talking about' before any authentication or data happens.

A single physical AP radio can host multiple SSIDs simultaneously, each one a separate WLAN with its own security settings and VLAN mapping. A small business AP might advertise 'Corp' (WPA2-Enterprise, VLAN 10), 'Guest' (WPA2-PSK + captive portal, VLAN 20), and 'IoT' (WPA2-PSK, VLAN 30) all at once. Each SSID gets its own BSSID (a virtual MAC address derived from the radio's base MAC).

Key exam facts: SSID is human-readable, broadcast in beacons every ~100 ms, up to 32 bytes, and one AP can host many SSIDs. Hiding the SSID (suppressing it in beacons) is NOT security — see entry 1.11.b.7.

hack.memory: SSID = the network's NAME TAG. Human-readable, printed on the business-card beacon that every AP hands out every 100 ms. One AP can wear many name tags — 'Corp', 'Guest', 'IoT' — each with a different VLAN and security.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'What does SSID stand for, and how often is it broadcast?' Answer: Service Set Identifier, ~every 100 ms in beacon frames. On a Cisco WLC (sim or real), create a new WLAN and set the SSID. Connect a client and see the SSID appear in the scan list. Then create a second WLAN on the same AP and watch both appear.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. 'What is an SSID?' is the easiest wireless question on the exam — free point if you know it.

1.11.b.info:
The BSSID (Basic Service Set Identifier) is the MAC address of the AP's radio interface that a client associates to. It uniquely identifies one AP's radio on one channel. Where the SSID is the human-readable network name, the BSSID is the machine-level fingerprint of the specific AP radio handling your traffic.

In a frame capture, you will see:

  • DA (Destination Address) — the recipient MAC (client or AP)
  • SA (Source Address) — the sender MAC
  • BSSID — the MAC identifying which BSS (AP) the frame belongs to

On a single AP broadcasting multiple SSIDs, each SSID has its own virtual BSSID — typically the radio's base MAC with the last hex digit(s) incremented per SSID. So SSID 'Corp' might use BSSID aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:00 and SSID 'Guest' uses aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:01 on the same radio.

Why this matters for roaming: when a client moves from one AP to another in an ESS (same SSID), the SSID stays the same but the BSSID changes. The BSSID is how the client (and network admin) knows exactly which AP is serving that client right now. In WLC logs and site surveys, BSSIDs let you track client movement AP by AP.

Exam takeaway: SSID = name (text), BSSID = AP radio's MAC (hex). If a question asks 'what uniquely identifies a specific AP radio?' the answer is BSSID.

hack.memory: SSID = name tag. BSSID = fingerprint. Two APs under one SSID = same name, different fingerprints. A virtual BSSID is just the radio MAC with the last hex nudged (.ee:00, .ee:01, .ee:02) per SSID.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Your client is connected to SSID "Corp" and roams from AP-1 to AP-2. What changes — SSID or BSSID?' Answer: BSSID changes, SSID stays the same. In Wireshark, capture Wi-Fi traffic and find the BSSID field in the 802.11 header; confirm it matches the AP's MAC.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Frequently paired with SSID on the exam — know both cold.

1.11.b.info:
A BSS (Basic Service Set) is the smallest building block of a Wi-Fi network: one AP and all the clients associated to it. The AP is the central coordinator; every client talks to the AP, not directly to other clients. The BSS is identified by its BSSID — the AP radio's MAC address.

A BSS is essentially a single RF 'cell' — all traffic inside it is relayed through the one AP. If client A wants to send to client B in the same BSS, the frame goes client-A → AP → client-B, never directly A → B. This is called the infrastructure mode of 802.11 and is the default.

A BSS has a physical boundary: the BSA (Basic Service Area), which is the area where clients can hear the AP's signal strongly enough to associate. Outside the BSA, clients either roam to a neighboring AP (if there is one in the same ESS) or lose connection.

Contrast with:

  • IBSS (Independent BSS) — ad-hoc mode, no AP, clients talk directly peer-to-peer. Rare in production.
  • ESS (Extended Service Set) — two or more BSSes sharing the same SSID, stitched together by a distribution system (wired LAN) to enable roaming.

Exam trap: don't confuse BSS (the logical AP + clients) with BSSID (the AP's MAC). BSS is the group; BSSID is the identifier of the group.

hack.memory: BSS = 'AP's club.' One AP, many clients, everybody talks through the AP (no direct client-to-client). The club's name on the door is the BSSID — the AP's MAC.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'In a BSS with one AP and 5 clients, can client-A send directly to client-B?' Answer: no — all frames go through the AP (infrastructure mode). Draw a BSS on paper: one AP in the middle, clients around it, arrows all going to/from the AP.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. BSS, IBSS, ESS is a three-term set — memorize the distinctions together.

1.11.b.info:
An ESS (Extended Service Set) is two or more BSSes connected by a distribution system (typically a wired LAN or a WLC-managed network) that advertise the same SSID. The ESS is what enables seamless roaming: a client can move from AP-1 to AP-2 to AP-3 without losing its connection, because all three APs are part of the same ESS sharing the same SSID.

From the client's perspective, an ESS looks like one big Wi-Fi network. The SSID never changes. Behind the scenes, the client is actually leaving one BSS and joining another — the BSSID changes with every roam, but the user does not notice.

How the distribution system connects it all: each AP in the ESS has a wired uplink to a switch. That switch hands client traffic into the LAN (or tunnels it to a WLC). When client-A on AP-1 talks to client-B on AP-2, the frame goes client-A → AP-1 → switch → AP-2 → client-B. The wired LAN is the 'distribution system' stitching the BSSes together.

Roaming mechanics: a client decides to roam based on signal strength and data rate, not the AP. The AP cannot force a roam (outside of 802.11k/r/v assisted roaming). When the client's signal to AP-1 drops and signal to AP-2 is stronger, the client sends a reassociation request to AP-2. AP-2 accepts, AP-1 is notified via the distribution system, and traffic shifts. Fast-roaming extensions (802.11r) pre-negotiate keys so encryption does not need to restart.

Exam fact: ESS = multiple APs, same SSID, different BSSIDs, seamless roaming via the distribution system.

hack.memory: ESS = 'the franchise.' Many storefronts (BSSes), one brand name (SSID), different addresses (BSSIDs), connected by the back office (distribution system). You walk from one to the next and never notice you changed stores.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'You walk through an office and your laptop stays connected to SSID "Corp" the whole time. What wireless construct is this?' Answer: ESS (Extended Service Set). Draw three APs on a floor plan, label them BSS-1/2/3, label their BSSIDs, then show an arrow labeled 'client roams' crossing them while the SSID stays 'Corp'.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. BSS/ESS/IBSS is a classic three-way question — expect one on the exam.

1.11.b.info:
A beacon frame is a management frame that an AP transmits periodically — by default every 100 ms (10 beacons per second, TBTT or Target Beacon Transmission Time). Its job is to announce the network's presence to any client in range. Every beacon is a small broadcast packet containing the network's parameters.

Contents of a beacon include:

  • SSID — the network name (or null if the SSID is hidden)
  • BSSID — the AP radio's MAC address (in the frame header)
  • Supported rates — e.g., 6, 9, 12, 18, 24, 36, 48, 54 Mbps
  • Capabilities — supported features like short preamble, privacy (encryption), short slot time
  • Timestamp — TSF (Timing Synchronization Function) value so clients can sync timers
  • DTIM — Delivery Traffic Indication Map for power-save clients
  • Security parameters — WPA/WPA2/WPA3 RSN (Robust Security Network) info

Clients doing a passive scan simply sit on a channel and collect beacons. Clients doing an active scan send probe requests and get probe responses that look almost identical to beacons. Either way, beacons are how the client builds its list of available networks.

Hidden SSID: the AP can omit the SSID from the beacon's SSID element (leaving it null or zero-length). The beacon is still sent, so the AP is not invisible — any scanner can see the BSSID, channel, and capabilities. The SSID just leaks in other management frames (probes, associations). This is why hiding the SSID is not security.

hack.memory: Beacon = 'lighthouse flash.' Every 100 ms the AP shouts 'I'm here, I'm Corp-WiFi, I speak 6-54 Mbps, I require WPA2, I'm on channel 6.' Clients listening on the channel catch the flash and learn the network.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'How often does an AP send beacon frames by default?' Answer: every ~100 ms (10 times per second). In Wireshark on a Wi-Fi interface, capture and filter on `wlan.fc.type_subtype == 0x08` — you will see beacons streaming in. Inspect one and identify SSID, BSSID, supported rates, and RSN.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Beacons come up in questions about passive vs. active scanning and about hidden SSIDs.

1.11.b.info:
A single physical AP radio can advertise multiple SSIDs at the same time, each one backed by its own WLAN profile. On a Cisco WLC, each WLAN profile maps an SSID to a VLAN, a security policy, and optional QoS/policy rules. The AP radio hosts all of them by transmitting multiple beacons (one per SSID) on the same channel and giving each SSID its own virtual BSSID.

Typical enterprise split:

  • Corp — SSID for employees, WPA2/3-Enterprise via 802.1X/EAP, mapped to VLAN 10, full internal access
  • Guest — SSID for visitors, WPA2-PSK or open with captive portal, mapped to VLAN 20, internet-only
  • IoT — SSID for printers, cameras, sensors, WPA2-PSK, mapped to VLAN 30, isolated from internal servers
  • Voice — SSID for Wi-Fi phones, WPA2-Enterprise + QoS, mapped to VLAN 40

The client picks which SSID to join; the AP silently enforces the matching VLAN tag and security policy on every frame. From the user's perspective, it feels like 4 different Wi-Fi networks even though it is one AP.

Cost of many SSIDs: each extra SSID means an extra beacon every 100 ms. Too many SSIDs (say, 8+) eats airtime overhead and reduces capacity for real data. Best practice: limit to 3-5 SSIDs per radio.

Exam takeaway: one AP can broadcast multiple SSIDs, and each SSID can be its own VLAN with its own security. This is how guest networks coexist with corporate networks on the same hardware.

hack.memory: One AP, many SSIDs = 'food truck with a menu.' Same truck, different meals, different prices, different ingredients — but only one window and one cook. Airtime is the catch: too many menu items slows every order.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Can one AP host a Corp SSID on VLAN 10 and a Guest SSID on VLAN 20 at the same time?' Answer: yes — one AP can host many SSIDs, each mapped to a different VLAN and security profile. In Packet Tracer or a WLC lab, create two WLANs on the same AP and connect two clients — one to each SSID — and trace the VLAN tags on the uplink trunk.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55 / Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25-26. Multi-SSID deployment is a common exam scenario, especially with guest networks.

1.11.b.info:
A hidden SSID is an AP configured to NOT include the SSID name in its beacon frames — the SSID element is either empty or zero-length. The goal, in theory, is to make the network invisible to casual scanners. In practice, this is security theater, not security.

Why it does not work: the SSID still leaks in several other 802.11 management frames:

  • Probe requests — when a client is configured to auto-connect to a hidden SSID, it actively broadcasts the SSID name in probe requests ('hey, is anyone out there named Corp?'). Anyone sniffing the air sees the probe and learns the SSID.
  • Probe responses — when the AP replies to a probe, the SSID is included.
  • Association requests — when the client joins, the SSID is in the association request.
  • Reassociation requests — during roaming, same deal.

So the 'hidden' SSID is exposed the moment any client tries to use it. A wireless sniffer (Wireshark, Kismet, Airodump-ng) reveals it in seconds. Cisco and the CCNA explicitly state that hiding the SSID does NOT provide security.

What actually secures a WLAN: strong encryption (WPA2 or WPA3), strong authentication (802.1X for enterprise, a long random PSK for home), and Protected Management Frames (PMF, mandatory in WPA3). The SSID being visible or hidden is irrelevant to security.

Downside of hiding: clients configured for hidden SSIDs actively probe everywhere they go, effectively broadcasting the SSID to every coffee shop they visit. It is worse than pointless — it leaks the SSID more, not less.

hack.memory: Hidden SSID = 'taking the sign off the restaurant.' Google Maps still lists the address, customers still ask 'hey, you open?' out loud, and the waiter still shouts the name. It's not a disguise — it's just inconvenient for you and transparent to attackers.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Does hiding the SSID provide security?' Answer: NO. The SSID leaks in probe requests, probe responses, association requests, and reassociation requests. In Wireshark, capture 802.11 traffic near a 'hidden' SSID and filter for probe requests — the SSID will be plainly visible.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55 / Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25-26. 'Is hidden SSID security?' is nearly guaranteed on the exam. The answer is always no.

1.11.c.info:
The two primary Wi-Fi bands on the CCNA are 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz, and they trade off along three axes: range, throughput, and interference.

2.4 GHz advantages:

  • Longer range — lower frequency = longer wavelength = better propagation. A 2.4 GHz signal easily reaches 30-45 m indoors.
  • Better wall penetration — drywall, glass, and wood absorb less 2.4 GHz energy than 5 GHz.
  • Universally supported — every Wi-Fi device supports 2.4 GHz; some legacy IoT devices only support 2.4 GHz.

2.4 GHz disadvantages:

  • Only 3 non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11) — crippling in dense deployments.
  • Lots of non-Wi-Fi interference — microwaves, Bluetooth, baby monitors, Zigbee.
  • Lower maximum throughput — older 802.11n/g standards, narrower usable bandwidth.

5 GHz advantages:

  • 24+ non-overlapping channels — dense deployments work.
  • Much less interference — almost no non-Wi-Fi devices live here.
  • Higher throughput — channel bonding up to 160 MHz, modern standards (ac, ax) run primarily on 5 GHz.

5 GHz disadvantages:

  • Shorter range — roughly 2/3 the reach of 2.4 GHz.
  • Worse wall penetration — more APs needed for the same coverage area.

Design rule: use both bands (dual-band APs), let clients auto-select. Enterprise design leans on 5 GHz for capacity and uses 2.4 GHz as backup coverage. Some WLCs steer capable clients toward 5 GHz ('band steering').

hack.memory: 2.4 = 'truck' — big, slow, drives through walls. 5 = 'sports car' — fast, clean roads, stops at walls. Enterprise uses both: 5 GHz is the highway for capacity, 2.4 GHz is the gravel road for coverage.
hack.practice: Build a comparison table: axis | 2.4 GHz | 5 GHz. Rows: range, penetration, non-overlap channels, interference, max throughput. Drill until instant. Flashcard: 'Which band has more non-overlapping channels?' → 5 GHz. 'Which has better range?' → 2.4 GHz. 'Which has less interference?' → 5 GHz.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. The 2.4-vs-5 tradeoff is a guaranteed exam question. Know all three axes cold.

1.11.c.info:
dBm (decibels relative to 1 milliwatt) is a logarithmic unit for measuring RF signal strength. Wi-Fi RSSI (Received Signal Strength Indicator) is reported in dBm. Because it is logarithmic and referenced to 1 mW, the values are negative for any received signal, and closer to zero means stronger.

Reference scale (memorize):

  • -30 dBm — Excellent. You are essentially standing on top of the AP.
  • -50 dBm — Good. Normal indoor strong signal.
  • -60 dBm — Acceptable for most traffic.
  • -67 to -70 dBm — Minimum for reliable data and voice.
  • -80 dBm — Very weak. Connections drop frequently.
  • -90 dBm — Noise floor. Effectively unusable.

How to read the scale intuitively: the 'loudness' of the signal doubles for every +3 dBm and is 10x for every +10 dBm. So -40 dBm is twice as strong as -43 dBm, and ten times as strong as -50 dBm. A drop from -60 to -70 is not '10 units weaker' — it is 10 times weaker in absolute power.

Why it is logarithmic: RF power spans many orders of magnitude. An AP transmits at +20 dBm (100 mW), and a receiver might hear it at -70 dBm (0.0000001 mW). Linear mW values would be unreadable. Logarithmic dBm compresses the range so humans can reason about it.

Exam focus: know that -30 is excellent, -60 is OK, -70 is minimum, and more negative = weaker. That is enough to answer every CCNA dBm question.

hack.memory: 'Less negative = louder.' -30 = shouting in your ear. -70 = whispering across the room. -90 = someone mouthing words in another building. The 3/10 rule: +3 dB doubles power, +10 dB is 10x.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'What is a weak but still usable Wi-Fi signal in dBm?' → -70. 'Which is stronger: -50 or -60?' → -50 (closer to zero). On your phone, install a Wi-Fi analyzer and walk around your house — watch RSSI drop as you move farther from the AP. Find the dead zone at -80 and below.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. dBm values appear in troubleshooting questions — 'the client sees -82 dBm, what should you do?' (move closer / add an AP / increase TX power).

1.11.c.info:
The 3 dB rule is the rule of thumb for converting between logarithmic dB changes and linear power ratios. Because dB is a log scale (base 10), every 3 dB step corresponds to roughly a factor of 2 in power.

Quick reference:

  • +3 dB = ~2x power (doubled)
  • -3 dB = ~1/2 power (halved)
  • +10 dB = 10x power
  • -10 dB = 1/10 power
  • +6 dB = ~4x power (two doublings)
  • +20 dB = 100x power (two tens)

Combine them: +13 dB = +10 dB + 3 dB = 10 × 2 = 20x power. +16 dB = 10 × 2 × 2 = 40x power. -7 dB = -10 +3 dB ≈ 1/5 power. This saves time when comparing signal strengths without a calculator.

Applied to RSSI: a client at -50 dBm has twice the received power of a client at -53 dBm, four times the power at -56 dBm, and ten times the power at -60 dBm. Even 'only 10 dB' between two readings is a meaningful 10x swing in how much energy is hitting the receiver.

Applied to antenna gain: an antenna with 6 dBi of gain focuses 4x the power in the desired direction compared to an isotropic (0 dBi) antenna. Doubling antenna gain = +3 dB. Upgrading from a 2 dBi to an 8 dBi antenna is a 4x-power boost in the covered direction.

Applied to AP TX power: going from 20 dBm (100 mW) to 17 dBm halves your output; going from 20 dBm to 23 dBm doubles it. RRM's TPC uses these 3 dB steps when tuning.

hack.memory: 'Three doubles, ten tens.' +3 dB = double the power, +10 dB = ten times. Subtract for the reverse. That's it. With these two rules you can compute any dB change mentally.
hack.practice: Flashcard drill: '+6 dB is how many times stronger?' (4x). '-6 dB?' (1/4). '+13 dB?' (20x). '+20 dB?' (100x). For Wi-Fi troubleshooting: 'Going from -60 to -63 dBm — how much weaker?' (half).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. CCNA rarely forces the exact calculation, but the 3 dB rule lets you reason about 'which signal is stronger' questions instantly.

1.11.c.info:
SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio) is the difference (in dB) between your desired signal strength and the background noise floor on the same channel. It answers the only question that matters for link quality: can the receiver hear me above the noise?

Calculation: SNR = |signal_dBm - noise_dBm|. Example: signal -60 dBm, noise floor -90 dBm. SNR = 30 dB. That is excellent. If the noise floor rises to -70 dBm (same signal), SNR drops to 10 dB — the link is in trouble even though the signal did not change.

Reference values:

  • ≥ 25 dB — Ideal. Supports high data rates (MCS 7+), voice, video, robust throughput.
  • 20 dB — Minimum for reliable data.
  • 15 dB — Degraded. Low data rates only, frequent retransmissions.
  • < 10 dB — Unusable. Modern modulation collapses, link falls back to the slowest rates or drops.

Why signal strength alone is misleading: a strong signal in a noisy environment can be worse than a weaker signal in a clean one. A warehouse with 200 IoT devices has a very high 2.4 GHz noise floor (say -75 dBm). Even a strong -50 dBm signal only gives 25 dB SNR. Move to 5 GHz where the noise floor is -95 dBm, and a weaker -65 dBm signal gives 30 dB SNR — better link.

Exam focus: know that SNR = signal - noise, 25+ is ideal, 20 is minimum, and SNR matters more than raw RSSI.

hack.memory: SNR = 'how loud is the speaker over the crowd?' Signal is the speaker, noise is the crowd. A whispering speaker in a quiet room (weak signal, low noise) beats a shouting speaker in a riot (strong signal, high noise). 25 dB SNR = clear speech, 20 dB = OK, under 10 dB = silence.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Client sees -55 dBm signal and -80 dBm noise floor. What is the SNR, and is it usable?' Answer: 25 dB, excellent. Try another: signal -60, noise -70 → SNR 10 dB → poor. Practice the subtraction until it is reflex.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. SNR-based troubleshooting questions are increasingly common on the exam — 'strong signal but slow Wi-Fi' usually means high noise.

1.11.c.info:
802.11n (Wi-Fi 4, 2009) was the first major multi-band Wi-Fi standard, operating on both 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz simultaneously. It introduced the technologies that still define modern Wi-Fi performance.

Key innovations:

  • MIMO (Multiple Input, Multiple Output) — uses multiple antennas to send multiple parallel data streams (spatial streams) over the same channel. Up to 4 streams in 802.11n. Each extra stream roughly multiplies throughput.
  • 40 MHz channel bonding — combines two adjacent 20 MHz channels in 5 GHz to double throughput (less common in 2.4 GHz due to lack of space).
  • Frame aggregation (A-MPDU / A-MSDU) — packs multiple frames into one transmission, reducing header overhead.
  • Short Guard Interval (SGI) — reduces the gap between symbols from 800 ns to 400 ns, boosting throughput ~11%.

Maximum throughput: up to 600 Mbps theoretical with 4 spatial streams, 40 MHz channel, short guard interval. Real-world single-client: ~150-300 Mbps in good conditions.

Backward compatibility: 802.11n can operate in 'mixed mode' with 802.11a/b/g clients, at the cost of efficiency. Pure 'greenfield' 802.11n-only mode is faster but breaks old clients.

Exam identity: 'MIMO' is the 802.11n headline feature. If the exam asks 'which Wi-Fi standard introduced MIMO?' the answer is 802.11n / Wi-Fi 4. Also: 2.4+5 GHz, up to 600 Mbps.

hack.memory: 802.11n = 'N for New MIMO.' First standard with Multiple In, Multiple Out antennas. Dual-band, 600 Mbps peak. Wi-Fi generation name: 4.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Which 802.11 standard first introduced MIMO, and what Wi-Fi generation is it?' Answer: 802.11n, Wi-Fi 4. 'What bands?' 2.4 and 5 GHz. 'Peak theoretical speed?' 600 Mbps. Build a Wi-Fi generation table (a, b, g, n, ac, ax) and drill it.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. The Wi-Fi generation table is guaranteed exam material. 802.11n → Wi-Fi 4 → MIMO.

1.11.c.info:
802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5, 2013) pushed Wi-Fi into gigabit-class speeds. It is 5 GHz only (no 2.4 GHz support), which was a deliberate design choice to escape the crowded 2.4 GHz band and take advantage of wider channels.

Key innovations:

  • MU-MIMO (Multi-User MIMO) — downlink only — the AP can transmit to multiple clients simultaneously on different spatial streams. 802.11n MIMO was single-user (one client at a time); 802.11ac's MU-MIMO served up to 4 clients in parallel downlink.
  • 80 MHz and 160 MHz channel bonding — wider pipes for more throughput per client.
  • Up to 8 spatial streams (in Wave 2 APs).
  • 256-QAM modulation — more bits per symbol than 802.11n's 64-QAM, ~33% more throughput at the same SNR.
  • Beamforming standardized — AP focuses RF energy toward the client for better SNR.

Maximum throughput: up to ~3.5 Gbps (Wave 2) theoretical with 8 streams, 160 MHz, 256-QAM. Real single-client: ~500 Mbps to 1 Gbps depending on device. Some references cite 6.9 Gbps as the absolute theoretical max.

Wave 1 vs Wave 2: Wave 1 (2013) = 80 MHz, 3 streams, no MU-MIMO. Wave 2 (2016) = 160 MHz, 4+ streams, MU-MIMO downlink.

Exam identity: 802.11ac = 5 GHz only, introduced MU-MIMO (downlink), wider channels, Wi-Fi 5. If a question mentions '5 GHz only' or 'MU-MIMO downlink,' the answer is 802.11ac.

hack.memory: 802.11ac = 'AC for All Clients at once (downlink).' First standard with MU-MIMO (multi-user). 5 GHz only — skipped the crowded 2.4 GHz party. Wi-Fi 5.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Which 802.11 standard introduced MU-MIMO and operates in 5 GHz only?' Answer: 802.11ac / Wi-Fi 5. Compare to 802.11n: 'What does 802.11ac add?' Answer: MU-MIMO downlink, wider channels (80/160 MHz), 256-QAM, 5 GHz only.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Wi-Fi 5 and its MU-MIMO-downlink signature come up often.

1.11.c.info:
802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6, 2019) is the current enterprise standard and the focus of most modern WLAN deployments. Unlike 802.11ac, it operates in both 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz, and it is designed for dense environments — stadiums, airports, conference halls — where many clients are competing for airtime.

Key innovations:

  • OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access) — slices a channel into smaller Resource Units (RUs) so the AP can serve multiple clients on one channel simultaneously. Massive latency and efficiency win for dense deployments with many small packets.
  • MU-MIMO both uplink AND downlink — 802.11ac was downlink-only; 802.11ax adds uplink MU-MIMO so the AP can receive from multiple clients at once.
  • BSS Coloring — tags frames with a 'color' per BSS. If an AP hears a frame from a different color, it knows it is another network and treats it differently (often ignores it), improving spatial reuse in dense deployments.
  • TWT (Target Wake Time) — lets battery-powered clients negotiate specific wake schedules, dramatically reducing power consumption for IoT devices.
  • 1024-QAM — denser modulation, 25% more throughput than 256-QAM at good SNR.

Maximum throughput: up to ~9.6 Gbps theoretical. Real single-client: 1-2 Gbps common.

Exam identity: 802.11ax = Wi-Fi 6, OFDMA, BSS coloring, uplink + downlink MU-MIMO, 2.4+5 GHz. If the exam mentions OFDMA or BSS coloring, the answer is 802.11ax.

hack.memory: 802.11ax = 'AX for All-channel eXcellence.' OFDMA = 'airport TSA with multiple lanes, not one queue.' BSS coloring = 'jersey numbers so APs know which team they hear.' Uplink MU-MIMO = new in 6. 2.4+5 GHz (6E adds 6 GHz).
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Which 802.11 standard introduced OFDMA and BSS coloring?' Answer: 802.11ax / Wi-Fi 6. 'Does 802.11ax support uplink MU-MIMO?' Yes (802.11ac was downlink only). 'Bands?' 2.4 + 5 GHz (6 GHz added with Wi-Fi 6E).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Wi-Fi 6 is increasingly present on the exam; OFDMA and BSS coloring are the signature features.

1.11.c.info:
Wi-Fi 6E is 802.11ax extended into the 6 GHz band (5.925 to 7.125 GHz). The underlying technology is the same 802.11ax (OFDMA, BSS coloring, MU-MIMO, TWT) — the 'E' just means 'Extended' to the 6 GHz spectrum that regulators opened up in 2020-2021.

Why 6 GHz matters: the 6 GHz band adds 1200 MHz of new spectrum, enough for 59 new 20 MHz channels or 14 new 80 MHz channels or 7 non-overlapping 160 MHz channels. It is essentially a fresh, clean band with no legacy Wi-Fi devices on it yet — only Wi-Fi 6E and newer clients can use it.

Tradeoffs:

  • Cleaner spectrum — no legacy a/b/g/n/ac on 6 GHz, so no interference from older clients dragging the network down.
  • Shorter range than 5 GHz — higher frequency, worse penetration. 6 GHz works best as a capacity-layer in the same room as the AP.
  • Requires new hardware — a Wi-Fi 6 client cannot use the 6 GHz band. You need 'Wi-Fi 6E' explicitly printed on the device spec sheet.
  • Regulatory: 6 GHz is licensed-exempt (free) in the US but shared with incumbents (microwave backhaul, radar), requiring AFC (Automated Frequency Coordination) in outdoor/standard-power deployments.

Exam note: Wi-Fi 6E is mentioned in some CCNA study resources but is not a heavily tested exam topic at the time of this writing. Know it exists, know it extends 802.11ax to 6 GHz, and move on. The heavy hitters are 802.11n/ac/ax.

hack.memory: Wi-Fi 6E = 'Wi-Fi 6 + Extra band.' Same 802.11ax tech, new 6 GHz apartment with no neighbors. Short range, clean air. CCNA light on details — know it exists and that 'E = 6 GHz'.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'What does Wi-Fi 6E add over Wi-Fi 6?' Answer: 6 GHz band with ~1200 MHz of additional, clean spectrum. Same OFDMA / BSS coloring / MU-MIMO tech.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 55. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 25. Expect at most one conceptual question; do not over-invest. Wi-Fi 7 / 802.11be is beyond CCNA scope entirely.

1.11.d.info:
WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy, 1997) was the original 802.11 encryption standard. Its design goal was to provide privacy equivalent to a wired LAN — a goal it catastrophically failed to meet. WEP is completely broken and should never be used under any circumstance.

How WEP worked: a static pre-shared key (40-bit or 104-bit) was combined with a 24-bit IV (Initialization Vector) to form the per-packet encryption key for the RC4 stream cipher. The IV was sent in cleartext so the receiver could derive the same key.

Why it broke:

  • The 24-bit IV is far too short. On a busy network, the IV space (2^24 ≈ 16 million values) exhausts within hours, forcing IV reuse. Two packets with the same IV and the same key produce a solvable cryptographic relationship.
  • RC4 with weak keys — certain IVs produce statistically biased keystreams that leak key bytes. Attackers correlate enough samples to reconstruct the PSK.
  • Attacks like FMS (2001), PTW (2007), and chop-chop made WEP crackable in under 10 minutes with aircrack-ng and a laptop.
  • The integrity check (CRC-32) is linear and trivial to bypass, allowing packet injection.

Current state: WEP was deprecated by the IEEE in 2004 (802.11i ratified WPA2). Any modern AP that still supports WEP is considered actively insecure. Enterprise and home networks must use WPA2 at minimum.

Exam takeaway: if the question asks 'which Wi-Fi security is broken / uses RC4 / should never be used,' the answer is WEP. One guaranteed question.

hack.memory: WEP = 'Weak Encryption Protocol' (unofficial). RC4 with a 24-bit IV that cycles in hours. Cracked in minutes with aircrack-ng on a laptop since 2001. Any exam mention of WEP = 'this is the wrong answer / broken / never use'.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Which Wi-Fi encryption standard uses RC4 with a 24-bit IV and is considered broken?' → WEP. 'Approximate crack time for WEP?' → minutes. Drill the WEP → WPA → WPA2 → WPA3 evolution so you instantly pick WEP as the broken one.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56 (Wireless Security). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 26. WEP is the easy 'which is broken' question — almost guaranteed on the exam.

1.11.d.info:
WPA (Wi-Fi Protected Access, 2003) was the interim fix for WEP while the IEEE finalized the 802.11i standard (which became WPA2). WPA's design goal: add meaningful security while remaining deployable on existing WEP hardware via a firmware update.

Core change: TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol) replaced WEP's static key usage. TKIP still used RC4 (so old hardware could run it) but added:

  • Per-packet key mixing — a fresh key was derived for every packet from the base PSK plus a 48-bit sequence counter. No more static key reuse.
  • Michael MIC (Message Integrity Check) — a cryptographic checksum to detect tampering, replacing the broken CRC-32.
  • 48-bit sequence counter — protects against replay attacks.
  • 4-way handshake — established between client and AP during association to derive transient keys. This handshake persists in WPA2 and WPA3 (with variations).

Why WPA is deprecated: TKIP inherited weaknesses from RC4. Attacks like Beck-Tews (2008) and Ohigashi-Morii can decrypt small packets within minutes. TKIP is officially deprecated as of 802.11-2012; it should never be configured on new deployments. However, knowing it existed as the 'WEP patch' is still CCNA-testable.

Modes: WPA-Personal used a PSK (pre-shared key). WPA-Enterprise used 802.1X with EAP and a RADIUS server.

Exam identity: WPA = TKIP + RC4 + 4-way handshake = temporary patch between WEP and WPA2. If a question says 'TKIP,' it means WPA.

params.label: WPA — TKIP + RC4
params.status: Temporary fix for WEP. Now deprecated.

hack.memory: WPA = 'WEP Patched Anyway.' Still RC4, but wrapped in TKIP's per-packet key mixing and a new 4-way handshake. Good enough for 2003, broken by 2008, deprecated today. Never build new networks on TKIP.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'What cipher and encryption protocol does WPA use?' Answer: RC4 + TKIP. 'Which handshake did WPA introduce that WPA2 still uses?' Answer: the 4-way handshake. Compare WPA (TKIP/RC4) vs WPA2 (AES-CCMP) side by side.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 26. TKIP is a keyword — when you see it, think WPA (not WPA2).

1.11.d.info:
WPA2 (2004) is the ratification of IEEE 802.11i and has been the mandatory Wi-Fi security standard since 2006. It replaced TKIP with AES-CCMP, which is still considered strong today and is the minimum acceptable security for any production network.

AES-CCMP = Advanced Encryption Standard in Counter Mode with Cipher Block Chaining Message Authentication Code Protocol. Mouthful — the short version: AES provides the encryption, CCM provides the integrity check, all in one. AES replaces RC4; CBC-MAC replaces the Michael MIC.

Authentication modes:

  • WPA2-Personal (PSK) — all clients share a single pre-shared key. Easy for home and small office, but anyone with the password can decrypt all traffic they captured before getting the key. No per-user accountability.
  • WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X) — each user authenticates with unique credentials (typically username + password or certificate) via a RADIUS server. Generates per-user encryption keys. Required for corporate environments. Cisco-recommended.

Key features inherited / refined from WPA:

  • 4-way handshake — derives PTK (Pairwise Transient Key) and GTK (Group Temporal Key) from the PSK or EAP-derived PMK.
  • Replay protection via packet number (PN) counters.

Known weaknesses: WPA2-PSK is vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks — if an attacker captures the 4-way handshake, they can brute-force the PSK offline at their leisure. A strong (long, random) PSK mitigates this but does not eliminate it. This is what WPA3 set out to fix.

KRACK (2017) was a protocol-level flaw in the 4-way handshake's key reinstallation logic, patched quickly in client and AP firmware; WPA2 itself remains deployable.

Exam identity: WPA2 = AES-CCMP = current minimum = either Personal (PSK) or Enterprise (802.1X/RADIUS).

hack.memory: WPA2 = 'Actual security.' AES-CCMP replaces TKIP/RC4. Two flavors: Personal (PSK) for home, Enterprise (802.1X + RADIUS) for corp. Weak spot: offline dictionary attack on captured handshake — WPA3 fixes this.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'What cipher does WPA2 use, and what does CCMP stand for?' Answer: AES, and Counter Mode with Cipher Block Chaining MAC Protocol. 'What WPA2 mode uses RADIUS?' Enterprise (802.1X). In a WLC lab, create one WPA2-PSK WLAN and one WPA2-Enterprise WLAN — confirm only Enterprise prompts for RADIUS server info.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 26. WPA2 + AES is the single most important wireless security fact on the exam.

1.11.d.info:
WPA3 (2018) is the newest Wi-Fi security standard, designed to fix the structural weaknesses of WPA2 — especially the offline dictionary attack on WPA2-PSK handshakes. Its headline feature is SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals), which replaces the PSK 4-way handshake with a Dragonfly key exchange.

What SAE gives you:

  • Forward secrecy — each session uses a fresh, derived key. If an attacker records your encrypted traffic today and steals the PSK next year, they still cannot decrypt the old traffic. WPA2 does not have this.
  • Resistance to offline dictionary attacks — SAE's handshake mathematically requires an online interaction per password guess. An attacker who captures the handshake cannot brute-force the password on their own computer. This is the biggest single improvement over WPA2-PSK.
  • Equivalent ease of use — from the user's perspective, you still enter a password; the cryptography is invisible.

WPA3 modes:

  • WPA3-Personal — uses SAE instead of PSK. Same UX, much stronger security.
  • WPA3-Enterprise — still uses 802.1X/EAP, but mandates stronger ciphers and optional 192-bit security suite (Suite B) for government/finance.

Other WPA3 features:

  • PMF (Protected Management Frames) — mandatory. WPA2 made it optional; WPA3 makes it required. PMF encrypts deauth/disassoc frames so attackers cannot spoof kicks to force clients to reconnect.
  • OWE (Opportunistic Wireless Encryption) — encrypts open/guest networks without a password, replacing 'no security' with 'at least the air is encrypted.' Not SAE; a separate feature.

Exam identity: WPA3 = SAE (replaces PSK) + forward secrecy + mandatory PMF + OWE for open networks. If the exam asks which standard resists offline dictionary attacks, it is WPA3.

params.label: WPA3 — SAE + AES (mandatory PMF)
params.status: Newest. Forward secrecy. Offline dictionary-attack resistant.

hack.memory: WPA3 = 'Safe Against Eavesdroppers.' SAE replaces PSK with a Dragonfly handshake that: (1) gives forward secrecy and (2) blocks offline brute-force. Plus mandatory PMF (no more deauth spoofing) and OWE (encrypted open networks).
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'What replaces PSK in WPA3-Personal?' Answer: SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals, aka Dragonfly). 'What are two things SAE provides that WPA2-PSK does not?' Forward secrecy and offline-dictionary-attack resistance. 'Is PMF mandatory in WPA3?' Yes.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 26. The 'why WPA3?' question is fair game — memorize SAE + forward secrecy + offline attack resistance.

1.11.d.info:
WPA2-Personal, also known as WPA2-PSK (Pre-Shared Key), is the simpler of the two WPA2 modes. The entire network uses one shared password: every client types the same PSK to join, and everyone gets network access.

Where it is used:

  • Home Wi-Fi networks (the default for every home router)
  • Small office Wi-Fi (under ~20 users)
  • Guest networks and temporary deployments
  • IoT device networks where the devices cannot do 802.1X

How the key exchange works:

  1. Both sides already know the PSK.
  2. During the 4-way handshake, the client and AP derive a Pairwise Master Key (PMK) from the PSK + SSID, then a per-session Pairwise Transient Key (PTK).
  3. The PTK is used to encrypt unicast traffic with AES-CCMP.
  4. A Group Temporal Key (GTK) is distributed for multicast/broadcast.

Weaknesses:

  • No per-user accountability — everyone uses the same password. You cannot revoke one user without changing everyone's password.
  • Offline dictionary attack — an attacker who captures the 4-way handshake can brute-force the PSK offline. A weak PSK ('Summer2024!') falls in seconds. A long random PSK resists this attack but does not eliminate it. WPA3-Personal (SAE) is the structural fix.
  • Compromise spreads — if any user leaks the PSK, everyone is exposed.

Best practice: use a long (20+ character) random PSK; rotate it when staff changes. For anything larger than a small office, use WPA2-Enterprise instead.

hack.memory: WPA2-PSK = 'one key, everyone in.' Great for home, bad for enterprise (no per-user tracking, no revocation without rotating the password for all). Weak point: offline dictionary attack on the captured handshake — use a LONG random PSK.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'What handshake does WPA2-PSK use, and what is its main weakness?' Answer: 4-way handshake, offline dictionary attack on captured handshake. 'When is WPA2-PSK appropriate?' Home/small office/guest/IoT. In a WLC or home router lab, configure WPA2-PSK and test that all clients use the same password.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 26. 'Personal vs Enterprise' is a common exam pairing — know the tradeoffs cold.

1.11.d.info:
WPA2-Enterprise uses the IEEE 802.1X framework with EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol) to authenticate each user individually, typically against a central RADIUS server (Cisco ISE, Microsoft NPS, FreeRADIUS). Every user gets unique credentials and unique encryption keys — no shared password.

Three roles in 802.1X:

  • Supplicant — the client device (laptop, phone) that requests access.
  • Authenticator — the AP or switch enforcing access. Passes EAP messages between supplicant and authentication server. Does not make the decision itself.
  • Authentication Server — the RADIUS server that actually validates credentials and says 'accept' or 'reject' to the authenticator.

Common EAP methods:

  • EAP-TLS — mutual certificate-based authentication. Most secure, hardest to deploy (PKI required). Gold standard for enterprise.
  • PEAP (Protected EAP) — builds a TLS tunnel using only a server certificate, then authenticates the user inside with MSCHAPv2 (username/password). Easier to deploy; most common in Microsoft environments.
  • EAP-FAST — Cisco-developed, uses a Protected Access Credential (PAC) instead of a cert. Less common outside legacy Cisco deployments.

Why Enterprise beats Personal:

  • Per-user authentication — revoke one user without affecting others.
  • Per-user encryption keys — every user has unique PMK/PTK, so one user's capture cannot decrypt another's traffic.
  • Full audit trail — RADIUS logs who connected, when, from what device.
  • Certificate or directory integration — tie Wi-Fi to Active Directory, LDAP, or Okta for single source of truth.

Cost: requires infrastructure — a RADIUS server, PKI (for EAP-TLS), and client configuration. Mandatory for any corporate network.

hack.memory: WPA2-Enterprise = 'real credentials, central auth.' Three-actor play: Supplicant (client), Authenticator (AP — just a passthrough), Auth Server (RADIUS — actually decides). EAP-TLS = certs (best), PEAP = TLS tunnel + user/pass (common), EAP-FAST = Cisco PAC (legacy).
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Name the three 802.1X roles.' Supplicant, Authenticator, Authentication Server. 'Which EAP method uses mutual certificates?' EAP-TLS. 'Which Wi-Fi mode generates per-user encryption keys?' WPA2-Enterprise. In a WLC lab, create a WPA2-Enterprise WLAN, point it at a RADIUS server IP, and walk through the EAP method selection.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 26. 802.1X concepts (supplicant/authenticator/auth server) and EAP methods are highly testable.

1.11.d.info:
OWE (Opportunistic Wireless Encryption) is a Wi-Fi feature introduced alongside WPA3 that brings encryption to open (no-password) networks. Traditionally, 'open' Wi-Fi (coffee shops, airports, hotels) is unencrypted — everything a user sends, including HTTP traffic and DNS queries, is visible to anyone sniffing the air. OWE fixes the air-side portion by encrypting the Wi-Fi link without requiring a password.

How it works: OWE uses a Diffie-Hellman key exchange during association to derive a per-client encryption key. The client and AP negotiate the key anonymously; no credentials or shared password are needed. Each client gets a unique key, so clients on the same open network cannot sniff each other's traffic.

What OWE does NOT provide:

  • Authentication — OWE does not verify the AP's identity. An attacker can still set up an 'evil twin' AP with the same SSID and clients will connect. HTTPS/application-layer security is still essential.
  • Protection against MITM on the air side if an attacker runs a rogue OWE AP.

OWE is a big upgrade over 'open' because it blocks casual passive sniffing — someone running Wireshark at the next table can no longer read your HTTP traffic. But it is not a replacement for WPA2/WPA3 with authentication.

Deployment: WPA3-capable APs advertise OWE on their 'open' SSID. WPA3-capable clients connect using OWE automatically. Legacy clients that do not support OWE can still connect in traditional unencrypted-open mode via 'OWE Transition Mode.'

Exam note: OWE is mentioned in CCNA prep but low-weight. Know: 'WPA3 feature that encrypts open networks without a password.' That single sentence covers the exam.

hack.memory: OWE = 'Open With Encryption.' No password required, but the air is encrypted anyway via Diffie-Hellman during association. Blocks passive sniffing at coffee shops — does NOT stop evil-twin APs. HTTPS still needed.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'What is OWE and which WPA generation introduced it?' Answer: Opportunistic Wireless Encryption, introduced with WPA3 — encrypts open networks without a password. 'Does OWE authenticate the AP?' No.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 26. Low-weight; memorize the one-sentence definition and move on.
